title: Pain de mie
subtitle: French soft sandwich loaf baked in a covered tin
excerpt: A French white sandwich loaf with a close, even crumb and a soft crust baked under a lid. Enriched with butter and milk, baked in a Pullman tin or a covered loaf tin, it stays fresh longer than an open-baked loaf and slices cleanly for sandwiches and toast.
sourceNotes: Pain de mie (literally bread of crumb, for its minimal crust) is documented in French culinary literature from at least the nineteenth century. The Pullman loaf, named for the railway sleeping car, is its American counterpart. The covered-tin method appears in early twentieth-century French baking manuals. The recipe here draws on the proportions in Auguste Escoffier, Le Guide Culinaire (1903) for an enriched French bread dough, adapted to the modern domestic Pullman tin. Escoffier's formula uses around 60% hydration with butter and milk as the enrichment, which is the standard today.

recipe.freezeNotes: Slice cool, freeze flat in a sealed bag. Toast from frozen.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Bakes well the night before. Store wrapped at room temperature for up to three days.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: conventional oven, lid on for the first 25 minutes. Remove lid and bake a further 10 minutes for a pale gold top.

glossaryTerms.definition: A crumb structure in which the air holes are small and evenly distributed throughout the loaf, rather than the large, open holes of a country sourdough or ciabatta. Pain de mie is built for a closed crumb so that sandwich fillings do not fall through. The covered tin prevents oven spring from opening the crumb structure during baking.

content.text: Pain de mie is an enriched white loaf baked in a covered tin to suppress the crust and keep the crumb tight. The lid prevents the loaf from doming, producing that characteristic squared-off shape, and the steam trapped inside gives the loaf its

. A Pullman tin with a slide-on lid is the correct tool; a standard loaf tin covered tightly with an oiled baking tray weighted with a cast-iron pan is a workable substitute. The enrichment of butter and milk keeps the loaf soft for longer than a plain white loaf.

Combine the flour, yeast, salt, and sugar in a large bowl or the bowl of a stand mixer, keeping salt and yeast apart before mixing. Add the warm milk and water and bring together to a rough dough. Add the butter in small pieces, working each piece in before adding the next. Knead by hand for 10 to 12 minutes or with the dough hook on medium for 8 minutes, until the dough is smooth, elastic, and pulls cleanly from the bowl. It will be slightly tacky but should not stick to your hands when lifted.
Shape the dough into a ball, place in a lightly oiled bowl, cover with a plate, and leave at room temperature for 90 minutes until nearly doubled. The dough should feel airy when pressed.

Lightly butter the tin and lid. Turn the dough out, press flat, and roll tightly into a log the length of the tin. Drop seam-side down into the tin and slide on the lid, or cover tightly with the improvised lid. Leave at room temperature for 45 to 60 minutes; the dough should rise to about 5 mm below the rim of the tin. Heat the oven to 210°C conventional. Bake covered for 25 minutes, then remove the lid and bake a further 10 minutes until the top is pale gold. An internal probe should read 94 to 96°C. Turn onto a rack and leave to cool fully before slicing.
